worth_it_explanation: Creative testing is the part of an account that gets the least attention once campaigns are live, and the cost lands in the gap between the ad that runs and the ad that could run. These checks read the account's own structure for the signal, whether an ad variation or experiment exists, whether each ad group carries more than one responsive search ad (RSA) to compare, and whether a clear winner from a past test was ever promoted. None of that needs a tool beyond the Google Ads interface, and the fixes are setup changes rather than rebuilds. An ad group running a single tired ad with no test in flight is leaving the account's best-performing copy undiscovered.
intro: Creative testing is how an account learns which ad copy actually performs rather than which copy the team assumed would. Google Ads offers two structured paths for this, ad variations for testing a single change across many campaigns, and custom experiments for testing larger changes on a slice of traffic, alongside the simpler practice of running more than one responsive search ad per ad group so the system has something to compare. These checks confirm that some form of testing is in flight, that each ad group has the creative to test, and that winners from past tests were acted on instead of left to expire.

audit_checks.title: No ad variation or experiment is testing creative in the account
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without a test in flight, the account never learns which headlines and descriptions perform best, so ad copy is set once and left. The cost is the difference between the running ad and a better one that is never found, which compounds across every impression the account serves.

educate.base:
Google Ads gives an account two structured ways to test ad copy. An ad variation takes a single change, such as swapping 'Free shipping' for 'Free returns' in a headline, and applies it across one campaign or the whole account so the change can be compared against the original. A custom experiment splits a slice of a campaign's traffic and runs a modified version alongside the original for a set period, which suits larger changes like a different bid strategy or a reworked set of ads.

When neither exists, the ad copy in the account was set at launch and has run unchallenged since. There is no read on whether a different headline order, a sharper call to action, or a new offer would convert better, so the account keeps serving the copy it started with. The system optimizes within the ads it has, but it cannot test ideas that were never entered.

The distinction matters when choosing a path. Ad variations are built for one change tested at scale. Custom experiments are built for one campaign tested with more than one change at once. A few questions help decide which the account needs:
  • Is the goal a single copy change across many ad groups, or a deeper change to one campaign?
  • Is there enough conversion volume in the target campaign for an experiment to reach a readable result?
  • Is the change a creative test, or a settings test that an experiment handles better?
You may want to start with an ad variation for a quick copy test and reserve experiments for changes that touch more than the ad text. Either one moves the account from running fixed copy to learning from it.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, creative tests tend to pay off fastest on the offer itself, where a headline testing 'Free shipping' against a price point or a promotion speaks directly to a buying decision. You may want to start an ad variation on the highest-spend shopping-adjacent search campaigns. A small lift on high-volume terms returns more than a large lift on a long-tail term that rarely serves.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, a strong test to run is the call to action and the promise around the form, for example 'Get a quote' against 'Talk to an expert', where the click commits the user to a longer step than a purchase. You may want to run an ad variation on the campaigns driving the most form submissions, so the result rests on real lead volume rather than a handful of conversions.

investigate.intro: Check whether any ad variation or custom experiment exists in the account, and whether it is active or has expired without a replacement.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open 'Campaigns', then the 'Experiments' page from the left navigation, and note whether any experiment is listed and whether its status is active, ended, or scheduled.
Open the ad variations view from the 'Experiments' page and note whether any variation is running, ended, or absent entirely.
If an experiment or variation shows as ended, read its dates. A test that ran months ago and was never followed by another means the account is back to serving fixed copy.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the account has any active ad variation, any active custom experiment, or neither, plus the end date of the most recent test if one has ended. An account with at least one active test passes. An account with no active test, or only tests that ended without a successor, is a fail recorded with the date of the last test if one existed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether an active ad variation or experiment exists, and the end date of the most recent ended test, to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} has no ad variation or custom experiment testing creative. The ad copy running today was set without a comparison against an alternative, so the account has no read on whether different headlines or a sharper offer would convert better, and it keeps serving the copy it launched with. {{voice_recommend}} starting an ad variation for a single copy change across the higher-spend campaigns, and reserving a custom experiment for any deeper change that touches more than the ad text. Set each test a clear end date and a metric to judge it on, so a winner can be acted on rather than left to expire.

audit_checks.title: A completed ad test produced a winner that was never applied to the campaign
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A test that ends without action wastes the traffic and budget the test consumed and leaves the weaker original running. The account paid to learn which ad performs better, then kept serving the loser, so the lift the test identified is never realized.

educate.base:
A creative test only returns value when its result is applied. An ad variation or a custom experiment that ends produces a comparison, the variant against the original, and Google Ads reports whether the difference is statistically meaningful. The next step is a decision, apply the winner, keep the original, or run a follow-up. A test that ends and sits unactioned leaves that decision unmade and the original copy still serving.

This shows up as an experiment marked 'ended' weeks or months ago with a clear result, where the winning variant was never applied to the campaign. The traffic and budget the test consumed bought a finding the account never used. The weaker ad keeps running, and the better ad the test identified is set aside.

The same pattern appears at the ad-group level without a formal experiment. When an ad group has run more than one responsive search ad for long enough to read, one usually carries a stronger Ad Strength and a better conversion rate, while the weaker ad keeps drawing impressions. Pausing the underperformer concentrates delivery on the ad that earns its impressions.

A few signs point to a result left unactioned:
  • An experiment shows 'ended' with a reported winner, but the campaign still runs the original ads
  • An ad group has two responsive search ads with a clear, sustained performance gap and neither has been paused
  • A past ad variation reached significance and was neither applied nor used to seed a follow-up test
You may want to close the loop on every ended test before starting a new one, so the account acts on what it already learned rather than testing the same idea twice.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, an unactioned winner often means the original ad keeps running through a peak shopping window when the tested variant would have converted better, so the cost lands on the highest-traffic days. You may want to apply ended winners ahead of a known seasonal push rather than after it, when the extra conversions are worth the most.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, the conversion volume is lower, so a test takes longer to reach a readable result and a finished result is worth acting on quickly. You may want to apply a winner as soon as the experiment reports significance. Restarting from scratch costs more weeks of lead volume than a store would lose.

investigate.intro: Find ended experiments and ad variations with a reported result, and confirm whether the winner was applied to the live campaign.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open the 'Experiments' page and filter to experiments with a status of 'ended'. For each, open it and read the reported result and whether a variant won.
For an ended experiment with a winner, open the original campaign and check whether the winning ads are the ones now serving. If the original ads still run, the result was not applied.
Open ad groups that run more than one responsive search ad, segment by ad, and note where one ad carries a clear, sustained edge in conversion rate or Ad Strength while the weaker ad still serves.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ended test, whether it reported a winner and whether that winner is applied to the live campaign, and per multi-ad ad group, whether a clear performance gap exists with the weaker ad still active. A test whose winner was applied, and an ad group with no unactioned gap, pass. An ended test with an unapplied winner, or an ad group with a clear gap and the weaker ad still running, is a fail recorded with the campaign or ad group name.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each ended test's result and whether its winner was applied, plus any multi-ad ad group with an unactioned performance gap, to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} ran a creative test to a conclusion but never applied the result, so the weaker ad is still serving. The traffic and budget the test consumed bought a finding the account is not using, and the lift the test identified goes unrealized while the original copy keeps running. {{voice_recommend}} applying the winning variant to the live campaign, and pausing any underperforming ad in an ad group where a sustained gap is already clear. Going forward, close out every ended test with a decision before starting the next one, so the account acts on what it has already learned.

audit_checks.title: Ad groups run a single responsive search ad with no second ad to test against
audit_checks.impact_rationale: With one ad per ad group, the account has no creative to compare and nothing to test, so there is no path to finding better copy short of replacing the only ad outright. A single ad also concentrates risk. A disapproval or a weak headline set has no backup to carry delivery.

educate.base:
Creative testing depends on having more than one ad to compare. An ad group that runs a single responsive search ad (RSA) gives Google Ads nothing to rotate against, so there is no read on whether a different headline mix or a reworked description would perform better. The system optimizes the assets inside that one ad, but it cannot compare it to an alternative that does not exist.

Google's own guidance recommends at least two responsive search ads per ad group with a 'Good' or 'Excellent' Ad Strength, partly so the account always has a second ad to test against and partly because a second strong ad can lift conversions in the ad group. A single-ad ad group also carries delivery risk. If the one ad is disapproved or limited, the ad group has no other ad to keep serving.

Adding a second ad is the entry point to every other test in this section. Once two ads run together, the account can read which performs better, pause the weaker one, and replace it with a new challenger, which turns the ad group into a running test rather than a fixed creative.

You may want to start with the ad groups that carry the most spend. A single ad on a high-volume ad group leaves the most performance on the table and benefits most from a second ad to compare.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, single-ad ad groups often sit on category or product-line terms that drive real revenue, so a second ad there opens testing on the copy that sells. You may want to add a second responsive search ad to the highest-revenue ad groups first, varying the offer or the value proposition so the test reads on a decision shoppers actually make.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, a single-ad ad group on a core service term has no backup if the one ad is limited, which can stall lead flow on a campaign the business depends on. You may want to add a second responsive search ad to the ad groups driving the most form submissions, both to protect delivery and to start testing the call to action.

learn_more.source_title: About Ad Strength for responsive search ads

investigate.intro: Find ad groups that run a single active responsive search ad, prioritizing the ones with the most spend.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open 'Campaigns', then 'Ads', and segment or group by ad group so each ad group's active ad count is visible.
Filter to ad type 'Responsive search ad' and ad status 'Enabled', then note every ad group showing a single enabled ad.
Sort the single-ad ad groups by cost so the highest-spend ones surface first. Those are where a second ad to test returns the most.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ad group, the count of enabled responsive search ads and the ad group's cost over a representative window. An ad group with two or more enabled responsive search ads passes. An ad group with a single enabled responsive search ad is a fail recorded with the ad group name and its cost, so the highest-spend single-ad ad groups rank first for a fix.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each ad group's count of enabled responsive search ads and its cost to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that several ad groups on {{site_name}} run a single responsive search ad, which leaves the account no second ad to test against and no backup if the one ad is limited or disapproved. With one ad per ad group, there is no path to finding better copy short of replacing the only ad outright. {{voice_recommend}} adding a second responsive search ad with a 'Good' or 'Excellent' Ad Strength to the highest-spend single-ad ad groups first, varying the headlines or the offer so the two ads form a readable test. Once two ads run together, pause the weaker one over time and replace it with a fresh challenger, so each ad group keeps testing rather than sitting on fixed copy.
